**Hugh Leo Carey** (April 11, 1919 – August 7, 2011) was an American politician and attorney of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") who served in the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") from 1961 to 1974 and as the 51st [governor of New York](/wiki/Governor_of_New_York "Governor of New York") from 1975 to 1982.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hugh_Carey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Carey was born in New York City, the son of Margaret ([née](/wiki/N%C3%A9e "Née") Collins) and Dennis Joseph Carey.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Among his siblings were brothers [Edward M. Carey](/wiki/Edward_M._Carey "Edward M. Carey") (former president of [Carey Energy Corporation](/wiki/Carey_Energy_Corporation "Carey Energy Corporation"))[[2]](#cite_note-NYTObit2002-2) and [Martin T. Carey](/wiki/Martin_T._Carey "Martin T. Carey") (an entrepreneur who owned [Winfield Hall](/wiki/Winfield_Hall "Winfield Hall") and [Bogheid](/wiki/Bogheid "Bogheid") in [Glen Cove](/wiki/Glen_Cove,_New_York "Glen Cove, New York") and [Seaview Terrace](/wiki/Seaview_Terrace "Seaview Terrace") in [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Rhode_Island "Newport, Rhode Island")).[[3]](#cite_note-Bender1975-3)

### Education and military service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hugh_Carey&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Education and military service")]

In 1939, Carey enlisted in the [New York National Guard](/wiki/New_York_Army_National_Guard "New York Army National Guard") as a private in C Squadron, [101st Cavalry](/wiki/101st_Cavalry_Regiment "101st Cavalry Regiment"). Later, he served as a major in the [104th Infantry Division](/wiki/104th_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "104th Infantry Division (United States)"), known as the "Timberwolves". He served in the [415th Infantry Regiment](/wiki/415th_Infantry_Regiment "415th Infantry Regiment") of the 104th Division as the Regimental S-3, operations officer.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Carey was with the 104th Division throughout its 10-month campaign in the [European Theater of Operations](/wiki/European_Theater_of_Operations,_United_States_Army "European Theater of Operations, United States Army"), which included the fighting in Northern France, Holland and Germany. His awards include the [Combat Infantryman Badge](/wiki/Combat_Infantryman_Badge "Combat Infantryman Badge"), the [Bronze Star](/wiki/Bronze_Star_Medal "Bronze Star Medal") with [Oak Leaf Clusters](/wiki/Oak_leaf_cluster "Oak leaf cluster"), and the [Croix de Guerre](/wiki/Croix_de_Guerre "Croix de Guerre") with Silver Star. He was discharged in 1946 at the rank of colonel.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

He received his B.A. from [St. John's University](/wiki/St._John%27s_University,_New_York "St. John's University, New York") in 1942. Following his military service, he enrolled at the institution's [law school](/wiki/St._John%27s_University_School_of_Law "St. John's University School of Law"), where he earned his J.D. in 1951. He was admitted to the New York state bar that same year.[[6]](#cite_note-timeline-6)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hugh_Carey&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Career")]

Carey was a partner in the law firm of [Finley, Kumble, Wagner, Underberg, Manley, Myerson & Casey](/wiki/Finley,_Kumble,_Wagner,_Underberg,_Manley,_Myerson_%26_Casey "Finley, Kumble, Wagner, Underberg, Manley, Myerson & Casey"). Carey became involved in politics following his wartime service. In 1946, he was named co-chair of a [New York State Democratic Committee](/wiki/New_York_State_Democratic_Committee "New York State Democratic Committee") youth division that was intended to attract young voters to support Democratic candidates.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### U.S. House of Representatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hugh_Carey&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: U.S. House of Representatives")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/cd/Hugh_L_Carey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hugh_L_Carey.jpg)

Carey as a congressman

In 1960, running as a [Democrat](/wiki/United_States_Democratic_Party "United States Democratic Party"), Carey was elected to the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") from a district generally centered around Brooklyn's [Park Slope](/wiki/Park_Slope,_Brooklyn "Park Slope, Brooklyn"), [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn") and [Bay Ridge](/wiki/Bay_Ridge,_Brooklyn "Bay Ridge, Brooklyn") neighborhoods, unseating Republican incumbent [Francis E. Dorn](/wiki/Francis_E._Dorn "Francis E. Dorn"). Carey's seven terms in office coincided with major demographic changes in his district, as exemplified by [deindustrialization](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt") and the decline of the Sunset Park waterfront's longstanding [breakbulk cargo](/wiki/Breakbulk_cargo "Breakbulk cargo") businesses; the concomitant emergence of [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") in eastern Park Slope; the coalescence of a nascent [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans "Puerto Ricans") community in western Park Slope and Sunset Park; and the opening of Bay Ridge's [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge") (which spurred migration by many [working-class](/wiki/Working-class "Working-class") whites in his district to the more suburban borough of [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island")) in 1964. Although he has been erroneously characterized as the first congressman to [oppose the Vietnam War](/wiki/Opposition_to_the_Vietnam_War "Opposition to the Vietnam War") (a stance actually taken by [Morningside Heights](/wiki/Morningside_Heights "Morningside Heights")-based congressman [William Fitts Ryan](/wiki/William_Fitts_Ryan "William Fitts Ryan")), he may have been the first member of the Brooklyn congressional delegation to speak out against the conflict following discussions with several of his children.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 1966, he was appointed Chairman of the Ad hoc Subcommittee on the Handicapped by [Adam Clayton Powell](/wiki/Adam_Clayton_Powell_Jr. "Adam Clayton Powell Jr."), then Chairman of the [House Education and Labor Committee](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Education_and_the_Workforce "United States House Committee on Education and the Workforce"). The sub-committee held hearings in Washington and New York City and Carey introduced HR 14. The "Carey Bill" provided, for the first time, a program of grants to the states for "initiating, expanding or improving education for children with disabilities. It also included other titles mirroring the structure of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act, PL 89–10, which Carey had assisted Powell in passing as part of the Lyndon Baines Johnson initiative.

Three parts of the Bill were picked up by the Senate: the grants to states, a new Bureau of Education for the Handicapped in the U.S. Office of Education and a National Advisory Committee. Carey's friend and mentor, John Fogerty of Rhode Island, the powerful Chairman of the Appropriations Subcommittee which provided funding for all Health, Education and Welfare programs, backed his legislation. The bill became Title VI of the ESEA, as Public Law 89-750, in 1966. Carey also sponsored and saw passed that year The Model Secondary School for the Deaf Bill, to be established on the campus of the [world's only liberal arts college for the deaf](/wiki/Gallaudet_University "Gallaudet University"). In 1965, he and Fogerty had sponsored The National Technical Institute for the Deaf, which was awarded to the Rochester Institute of Technology.

The Carey Bill and its grant program to the states began with a $2.5 million appropriation, to provide each state with $50,000 to plan for its implementation. The second year, the appropriation was $12.25 million, distributed to the states in proportion to their population. In 1975 Congress passed the Education for All Handicapped Children Bill, PL 94-142 which today distributes approximately $11 billion to the states for this purpose.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

He served on the [House Ways and Means Committee](/wiki/House_Ways_and_Means_Committee "House Ways and Means Committee") and led the effort to pass the first Federal Aid to Education program. He was elected Governor of New York in 1974 and resigned his Congressional seat on December 31, 1974.

### City politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hugh_Carey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: City politics")]

In 1969 Carey ran briefly for the Democratic nomination for Mayor. He then agreed to run for City Council President on the ticket led by former Mayor [Robert F. Wagner Jr.](/wiki/Robert_F._Wagner_Jr. "Robert F. Wagner Jr.") Carey narrowly lost the primary to incumbent City Council President [Francis X. Smith](/w/index.php?title=Francis_X._Smith&action=edit&redlink=1 "Francis X. Smith (page does not exist)"). Then he briefly mounted an independent bid for Mayor, from which he withdrew after the death of his two eldest sons in a car accident.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Governor of New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hugh_Carey&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Governor of New York")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Hugh_Carey_-_1977_NFTA_Report.jpg/250px-Hugh_Carey_-_1977_NFTA_Report.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hugh_Carey_-_1977_NFTA_Report.jpg)

Carey in 1977

In the [1974 gubernatorial election](/wiki/1974_New_York_gubernatorial_election "1974 New York gubernatorial election"), Carey became New York's first Democratic governor in 16 years, defeating [Howard Samuels](/wiki/Howard_J._Samuels "Howard J. Samuels") for the Democratic nomination and then unseating incumbent Republican [Malcolm Wilson](/wiki/Malcolm_Wilson_(governor) "Malcolm Wilson (governor)"), who had assumed the office after [Nelson Rockefeller](/wiki/Nelson_Rockefeller "Nelson Rockefeller") resigned in December 1973 to serve on the [Commission on Critical Choices for Americans](/wiki/Commission_on_Critical_Choices_for_Americans "Commission on Critical Choices for Americans"). Nationally, 1974 was dominated by the [Watergate scandal](/wiki/Watergate_scandal "Watergate scandal"), which ended President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon")'s presidency and hurt Republicans nationwide. In 1974, Democrats also recaptured the [New York State Assembly](/wiki/New_York_State_Assembly "New York State Assembly").

Carey is best remembered for his successful handling of New York City's economic crisis in the mid-1970s. Carey came into office with New York City close to bankruptcy and is credited with bringing business and labor together to help save New York City from the fiscal crisis. He managed to keep the growth of state spending below the rate of inflation through his frequent use of line-item vetoes and fights with the [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature"), which was at the time divided between a Republican-controlled [Senate](/wiki/New_York_Senate "New York Senate") and a Democratic-controlled [Assembly](/wiki/New_York_Assembly "New York Assembly").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Upon taking office, Carey cut taxes significantly, reducing corporate taxes from 14% to 10%, and capping personal income tax at 9%, and reducing capital gains taxes as well. His administration also offered tax credits to encourage new investment.[[13]](#cite_note-Wayne1982-13) As governor, he was responsible for building the [Jacob K. Javits Convention Center](/wiki/Jacob_K._Javits_Convention_Center "Jacob K. Javits Convention Center"); [Battery Park City](/wiki/Battery_Park_City "Battery Park City"); the [South Street Seaport](/wiki/South_Street_Seaport "South Street Seaport") and the economic development of New York City's outer boroughs. He also helped provide state funding for the construction of the [Carrier Dome](/wiki/Carrier_Dome "Carrier Dome") at [Syracuse University](/wiki/Syracuse_University "Syracuse University").[[14]](#cite_note-14) He is also remembered for preventing conservative legislators from reinstating the death penalty and preventing such legislators from taking away state abortion laws.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Photograph_of_President_Gerald_R._Ford_Meeting_with_Governor_Hugh_Carey_of_New_York_and_Mayor_Abraham_Beame_of_New_York_City_in_the_Cabinet_Room_to_Discuss_Federal_Financial_Aid_for_New_York_City_-_NARA_-_7582445.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photograph_of_President_Gerald_R._Ford_Meeting_with_Governor_Hugh_Carey_of_New_York_and_Mayor_Abraham_Beame_of_New_York_City_in_the_Cabinet_Room_to_Discuss_Federal_Financial_Aid_for_New_York_City_-_NARA_-_7582445.jpg)

Carey (L) and New York City mayor [Abraham Beame](/wiki/Abraham_Beame "Abraham Beame") (R) meet with President [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") at the White House in 1975 to discuss federal financial aid for New York City

Carey signed the Willowbrook Consent Decree,[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) which ended the hospitalisation of the [mentally ill](/wiki/Mentally_ill "Mentally ill") and [developmentally disabled](/wiki/Developmentally_disabled "Developmentally disabled"). His vision and leadership led to the community placement of the mentally ill and developmentally disabled, but also an increase of these people living on the streets. He also made major strides in community programs for the [mentally ill](/wiki/Mental_illness "Mental illness"). Carey also pardoned Cleveland "Jomo" Davis, one of the leaders of the [Attica prison riots](/wiki/Attica_prison_riots "Attica prison riots").

Carey's tenure in office was marked by a growing awareness of the environmental consequences of New York's strong industrial base, including the designation by the federal government of the [Love Canal](/wiki/Love_Canal "Love Canal") disaster area. Carey made environmental issues a priority of his administration. He was accused by Love Canal residents of being more concerned about the state's finances than the health of families living amidst one of the nation's most infamous environmental disasters.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Along with Senators [Ted Kennedy](/wiki/Ted_Kennedy "Ted Kennedy") and [Daniel Patrick Moynihan](/wiki/Daniel_Patrick_Moynihan "Daniel Patrick Moynihan") and [U.S. House Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") [Tip O'Neill](/wiki/Tip_O%27Neill "Tip O'Neill"), Carey led efforts to strengthen U.S. support for a political solution to the [Northern Ireland conflict](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_conflict "Northern Ireland conflict"). The four Irish-American politicians called themselves "The Four Horsemen."[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In 1977, he was an appellant in the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") case *[Carey v. Population Services International](/wiki/Carey_v._Population_Services_International "Carey v. Population Services International")*. Carey and his fellow appellants sought permission to continue enforcing laws prohibiting Population Services International (PSI) and others from distributing contraceptives to minors below 16 years old that had previously been struck down in a New York District Court. The Court upheld the lower court's determination that the law violated the [Due Process Clause](/wiki/Due_Process_Clause "Due Process Clause") of the [Fourteenth Amendment to the Constitution](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") and the rights of privacy afforded to individuals, including minors.

Carey considered running for president in [1976](/wiki/1976_United_States_presidential_election "1976 United States presidential election") and [1980](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election"). Carey's first wife had died in 1974, and Carey later attributed his decision not to seek the [Democratic nomination for president in 1976](/wiki/Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries,_1976 "Democratic Party presidential primaries, 1976") to her death. In [1978](/wiki/New_York_gubernatorial_election,_1978 "New York gubernatorial election, 1978"), he was challenged for re-election by State Assembly Minority Leader and former Assembly Speaker [Perry Duryea](/wiki/Perry_Duryea "Perry Duryea"). After a competitive, sometimes negative campaign, Carey was the first Democrat re-elected in 40 years. In 1980, he decriminalized homosexuality in New York State.

According to political scientist and author Daniel C. Kramer, "The Carey Administration had a hand in many important projects, the modernization of New York City's subways and the rescue of homeowners living in the Love Canal neighborhood being just two, before Carey committed several serious blunders which lowered voters' opinion of him that he decided not to run for reelection in 1982. Through it all, this man with the embarrassingly low poll ratings turned into a superb governor."[16](https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/375081.The_Days_of_Wine_and_Roses_Are_Over) On January 1, 1983, he was succeeded by his [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_New_York "Lieutenant Governor of New York"), [Mario Cuomo](/wiki/Mario_Cuomo "Mario Cuomo").

### Later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hugh_Carey&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Later career")]

In 1989, Carey announced that he was no longer pro-choice and regretted his support for legalized abortion and public financing of abortion as governor. In 1992, he joined other [anti-abortion](/wiki/Anti-abortion "Anti-abortion") leaders in signing the anti-abortion document "A New American Compact: Caring About Women, Caring for the Unborn."[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Later in his life, he was [of counsel](/wiki/Of_counsel "Of counsel") at the law firm of [Shea & Gould](/wiki/Shea_%26_Gould "Shea & Gould"). He continued to practice law as a member of the [Harris Beach](/wiki/Harris_Beach "Harris Beach") law firm and sat on the board of [Triarc Cos.](/wiki/Triarc "Triarc"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) the [Nelson Peltz](/wiki/Nelson_Peltz "Nelson Peltz") controlled holding company.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hugh_Carey&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

In 1947, Carey married Helen Owen (1924–1974). They became the parents of Alexandria, Christopher, Susan, Peter, Hugh Jr., Michael, Donald, Marianne, Nancy, Helen, Bryan, [Paul](/wiki/Paul_Carey_(politician) "Paul Carey (politician)"), Kevin, and Thomas. Beginning in 1961, the family resided at 61 Prospect Park West, a 1910 Park Slope mansion built for the daughter of [Bon Ami Company](/wiki/Bon_Ami_Company "Bon Ami Company") chairman and [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era") philanthropist William H. Childs; a decade later, Carey sold the home to journalist [Pete Hamill](/wiki/Pete_Hamill "Pete Hamill").[[21]](#cite_note-21) His wife, Helen, died of breast cancer in 1974. Peter and Hugh Jr. died in an automobile accident in 1969. Carey was devastated by the death of his wife and laid to rest any plans for the White House. Paul, who served as White House Special Assistant to President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") as well as 77th Commissioner of the [Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "Securities and Exchange Commission"), died of cancer in 2001.

In 1981, Carey married Evangeline Gouletas, a Chicago-based real estate mogul, just three months after meeting her.[[22]](#cite_note-22) This marriage proved controversial and a political liability. The marriage generated controversy due to false statements Gouletas made about her marital history. Initially, Gouletas claimed that she was a widow of a single marriage but later affirmed on the marriage license that she had two ex-husbands.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Gouletas also said that her first husband, with whom she had a daughter, was dead, but he was still alive at the time. In reality, she actually had three previous marriages, and all three of her former husbands were still living at the time. The marriage also caused trouble for Carey with the Catholic Church, since he married a thrice-divorced woman in a [Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church"); the church, which does not recognize civil no-fault divorce, refused to perform communion. To an extent, the marriage also hurt his public reputation. Carey and Gouletas divorced in 1989.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
Carey later described this marriage as "his greatest failure."[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology_54.jpg/250px-Rochester_Institute_of_Technology_54.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rochester_Institute_of_Technology_54.jpg)

Building 14 at the [Rochester Institute of Technology](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology "Rochester Institute of Technology") was named for Carey in 1984

Carey died surrounded by his family on August 7, 2011, aged 92.[[26]](#cite_note-HCObit2011-26) He was at his summer home on [Shelter Island, New York](/wiki/Shelter_Island_(town),_New_York "Shelter Island (town), New York").[[27]](#cite_note-NY1Death-27)

### Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hugh_Carey&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Legacy")]

On October 22, 2009, he was named as the recipient of [University at Albany Foundation](/wiki/University_at_Albany,_SUNY#Organization "University at Albany, SUNY")'s Citizen Laureate Award.[[28]](#cite_note-28) On October 22, 2012, the [Brooklyn Battery Tunnel](/wiki/Brooklyn_Battery_Tunnel "Brooklyn Battery Tunnel") was officially renamed the "Hugh L. Carey Tunnel".[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Building 14 at the [Rochester Institute of Technology](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology "Rochester Institute of Technology") was named for Carey in 1984.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
